subUnits.title: 7.1 Speech: Speaking to Persuade/Convince Continued
notes: 2. The Two Sides To An Argument: Proposing And Opposing
a. Corruption and the Nigerian state is endemic
1. Teacher uses real clock to time participants
2. Teacher leads students to introduce themselves and their topics
3. Teaches students to use the dictionary to learn definition
- Use persuasive language and logical arguments
diagramDescriptions: Chart showing debate structure and argument types
Sample debate topics with points for and against
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English persuasive speaking debate SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The two sides to an argument are:
quiz.explanation: The two sides to an argument are proposing (for) and opposing (against) a motion or topic.

quiz.q: Which is a current issue for debate?
quiz.explanation: Women are better leaders is a current issue suitable for debate and argument.

opts: Use persuasive language and logical arguments
quiz.explanation: Effective persuasion requires persuasive language, logical arguments, and evidence.

quiz.q: A rhetorical question is used to:
opts: Make a point without expecting an answer
quiz.explanation: Rhetorical questions are used to make a point or persuade without expecting a direct answer.

quiz.q: When gathering points to convince, you should:
quiz.explanation: Gathering points to convince requires presenting evidence, examples, and logical arguments.

quiz.q: Which topic is suitable for persuasive speaking?
quiz.explanation: Corruption and the Nigerian state is a current issue suitable for persuasive speaking and debate.

quiz.q: To introduce yourself in a debate, you should:
opts: State your name and topic clearly
quiz.explanation: In a debate, you should introduce yourself and your topic clearly to the audience.

quiz.q: Using a dictionary in debate preparation helps to:
quiz.explanation: Using a dictionary helps you learn definitions of key terms for accurate and effective argumentation.

quiz.q: Timing participants in a debate is important to:
opts: Ensure fairness and proper time management
quiz.explanation: Timing ensures fairness and proper time management so all participants have equal opportunity.
Addressing counterarguments means responding to and refuting opposing points effectively.

subUnits.title: 7.2 Structure: More on Adjectival Clauses
notes: - A dependent clause that modifies a noun or pronoun
2. Adjectival clauses qualify a noun or pronoun in the sentence
3. They are introduced by relative pronouns:
1. The man whose brother was sacked has committed suicide.
(whose brother was sacked = adjectival clause modifying man)
2. The song which Clara sang was very melodious.
(which Clara sang = adjectival clause modifying song)
3. The boy who won the prize is my friend.
(who won the prize = adjectival clause modifying boy)
4. The car that I bought is red.
(that I bought = adjectival clause modifying car)
3. Students make sentences with adjectival clauses
- Adjectival clauses always follow the noun they modify
- They cannot stand alone as complete sentences
- They provide additional information about a noun
diagramDescriptions: Chart showing relative pronouns and their uses
Sample sentences with adjectival clauses highlighted
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English grammar adjectival relative clauses SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.explanation: An adjectival clause modifies a noun or pronoun in a sentence.

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun is used for possession?
quiz.explanation: Whose is the relative pronoun used to show possession in adjectival clauses.

quiz.q: In the sentence The song which Clara sang was melodious, the adjectival clause is:
quiz.explanation: which Clara sang is the adjectival clause modifying the noun song.

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun is used for things?
quiz.explanation: Which is used for things or animals in adjectival clauses.

quiz.q: An adjectival clause is also called a:
quiz.explanation: An adjectival clause is also called a relative clause because it is introduced by relative pronouns.

quiz.q: In The man whose brother was sacked, whose refers to:
quiz.explanation: Whose shows possession and refers to the man (the mans brother).
Adjectival clauses are introduced by relative pronouns: who, whom, whose, which, that.

quiz.q: Which sentence contains an adjectival clause?
opts: The boy who won the prize is my friend.
quiz.explanation: who won the prize is an adjectival clause modifying boy.
Adjectival clauses always follow the noun or pronoun they modify.

quiz.q: Which relative pronoun can be used for both people and things?
quiz.explanation: That can be used for both people and things in adjectival clauses.

subUnits.title: 7.3 Comprehension: Reading to Test Speed and Accuracy
notes: - Colon: pause before explanation or list
1. Guides students to observe punctuation marks
4. Time reading exercises for speed improvement
- Dont subvocalize (dont say words in your head)
- Read in chunks, not word by word
- Use a pointer or finger to guide your eyes
diagramDescriptions: Chart showing punctuation marks and their functions
Sample passage with punctuation marks highlighted
Flowchart for improving reading speed and accuracy
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English reading speed accuracy comprehension SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.explanation: Question marks are used at the end of questions.
Full stops (periods) are used at the end of statements.
A comma indicates a short pause in a sentence.
A semicolon indicates a medium pause, longer than a comma but shorter than a full stop.

quiz.q: To increase reading speed, you should:
quiz.explanation: Reading in chunks (groups of words) rather than word by word increases reading speed.

opts: Saying words in your head while reading
quiz.explanation: Subvocalization is saying words in your head while reading, which can slow down reading speed.
A colon is used before an explanation, list, or quotation.

quiz.q: To improve reading accuracy, you should:
quiz.explanation: Paying attention to punctuation helps improve reading accuracy and comprehension.

quiz.q: Using a pointer while reading helps to:
opts: Guide your eyes and maintain focus
quiz.explanation: Using a pointer or finger helps guide your eyes and maintain focus while reading.
Frequent reading practice improves both reading speed and accuracy over time.

subUnits.title: 7.4 Vocabulary Development (Register): Politics
notes: Politics refers to the activities associated with governance, decision-making, and power distribution in a society.
12. Dividend - share of profits (in political context: benefits)
1. Teacher gives a story of a politician he/she knows
2. Teacher invites politician to give a talk
3. Use words in sentences and orally in discussions
- The opposition party criticized the government.
- The electorate went to the polls.
diagramDescriptions: Chart of political terms and definitions
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English vocabulary politics government SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.explanation: Democracy means government by the people, where citizens have the power to choose their leaders.
The opposition refers to political parties that are not in government.
A campaign refers to organized efforts to win elections or promote a political cause.
The electorate refers to all eligible voters in an election.
Polls refer to voting processes or opinion surveys.
A booth is a voting station where people cast their votes.
Independence means freedom from control or influence by others.

quiz.q: A poster in politics is used for:
quiz.explanation: A poster is used for campaign advertisement to promote candidates or parties.
Power in politics refers to the authority to govern and make decisions.
A governor is the head of a state government.

subUnits.title: 7.5 Continuous Writing: Argumentative Essay
notes: - Example: Mr. Chairman, Panel of Judges, Co-debaters, Ladies and Gentlemen
- Military rule is better than civilian rule
- Boarding school is better than day school
- Science has done more harm than good
diagramDescriptions: Chart of debate features and components
subUnits.youtubeQuery: English writing argumentative essay debate SS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Opening vocatives in a debate are used to:
quiz.explanation: Opening vocatives are formal greetings used to address the audience at the beginning of a debate.

quiz.q: The body of an argumentative essay should:
quiz.explanation: The body should present arguments logically with evidence and examples.
The conclusion should summarize main points and restate your position.
Addressing counterarguments means acknowledging and responding to opposing points.
Transitional words (firstly, secondly, moreover, however) help connect ideas smoothly.
A strong closing statement reinforces your position powerfully and leaves a lasting impression.

quiz.q: Evidence in an argumentative essay can be:
quiz.explanation: Evidence includes facts, statistics, examples, and expert opinions that support your arguments.

quiz.q: The tone of an argumentative essay should be:
quiz.explanation: The tone should be respectful and logical, even when disagreeing with opposing views.
In the introduction, you should state the topic and your position clearly.
Logical reasoning means using facts, evidence, and sound arguments to support your position.
